---
title: "TC Windspeed & 3RM Replication"
jupyter: ds-storm-impact
---

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
## Summary

We investigate whether cyclone population exposure (the number of people
living in areas exposed to different wind speed thresholds) can inform CERF
rapid-response allocation decisions for tropical cyclones. Using OCHA exposure
estimates at 34, 50, and 64 kt thresholds matched to CERF storm allocations
via IBTrACS, we address three objectives: **(1)** whether exposure alone
predicts CERF allocation amounts; **(2)** whether exposure predicts other
3RM outcome variables (funding required, people targeted, people affected);
and **(3)** whether adding exposure to the Rost (2025) 3RM regression model
improves its fit for storm allocations.

Results differ substantially by period. Over the full record (2006--2024),
correlations between exposure and allocation amounts are weak and mostly
non-significant. From **2016 onward** (the 3RM period), correlations
strengthen, with the 64 kt threshold showing the strongest relationship
(r² ≈ 0.19--0.43 depending on outlier treatment). However, even where
statistically significant, these low R² values mean exposure explains only
a small fraction of the variance in allocation amounts, limiting its
practical predictive utility. Exposure also significantly predicts
funding required and people affected in bivariate regressions (Objective 2),
though again with modest explained variance. When added to the full 3RM
model (Objective 3), exposure does not significantly improve fit in any
specification. CIRV (the vulnerability index) is not significant for
storm-only allocations. Small sample sizes (n = 29--40) constrain
statistical power throughout.
:::

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| output: false
import sys
sys.path.insert(0, "..")

import ocha_stratus as stratus
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from io import BytesIO
from scipy import stats as sp_stats
from great_tables import GT, loc, style
from plotnine import *
import warnings
from dotenv import load_dotenv

warnings.filterwarnings("ignore", module="plotnine")

load_dotenv()

from src.datasets.cerf import (
    build_analysis_dataset_api,
    remove_col_outliers_iqr,
)

df_ocha = stratus.load_parquet_from_blob(
    "ds-storm-impact-harmonisation/processed/adm0_ibtracs_exp_all.parquet"
)

merged_agg = build_analysis_dataset_api(df_ocha)

# Storm name lookup
engine = stratus.get_engine("prod")
all_sids = sorted(merged_agg["sid"].unique())
with engine.connect() as conn:
    _sid_names = pd.read_sql(
        f"SELECT sid, name FROM storms.ibtracs_storms "
        f"WHERE sid IN ({','.join(repr(s) for s in all_sids)})",
        conn,
    )
sid_name_map = dict(zip(_sid_names["sid"], _sid_names["name"]))
merged_agg["storm_name"] = merged_agg["sid"].map(sid_name_map)

print(f"Storm-country pairs: {len(merged_agg)}")
print(f"Unique storms: {merged_agg['sid'].nunique()}")
print(f"Year range: {int(merged_agg['allocation_year'].min())}–{int(merged_agg['allocation_year'].max())}")
print(f"Total CERF: ${merged_agg['total_usd'].sum():,.0f}")
```

## Preliminary: Distributions & Outliers

All variables are heavily right-skewed; log transformation is applied
throughout. Outliers are identified independently per wind speed column
using the IQR method on log₁₀-transformed values (Q1 − 2×IQR / Q3 + 2×IQR).

```{python}
#| label: distributions
#| fig-cap: "Distributions of key variables on raw and log scales. Raw data is heavily right-skewed; log transformation improves normality."
dist_rows = []
for col, label in [
    ("total_usd", "CERF Allocation (USD)"),
    ("pop_exp_34kt", "Pop Exposed ≥ 34 kt"),
    ("pop_exp_64kt", "Pop Exposed ≥ 64 kt"),
]:
    vals = merged_agg[col].dropna()
    vals = vals[vals > 0]
    for v in vals:
        dist_rows.append({"panel": f"{label} — Raw", "value": v, "scale": "Raw", "variable": label})
        dist_rows.append({"panel": f"{label} — Log₁₀", "value": np.log10(v), "scale": "Log₁₀", "variable": label})

dist_df = pd.DataFrame(dist_rows)
var_names = ["CERF Allocation (USD)", "Pop Exposed ≥ 34 kt", "Pop Exposed ≥ 64 kt"]
panel_order = [f"{v} — Raw" for v in var_names] + [f"{v} — Log₁₀" for v in var_names]
dist_df["panel"] = pd.Categorical(dist_df["panel"], categories=panel_order, ordered=True)

(
    ggplot(dist_df, aes("value"))
    + geom_histogram(bins=20, fill="#4292c6", color="#666666", alpha=0.8)
    + facet_wrap("~panel", scales="free", ncol=3)
    + labs(x="", y="Count", title="Distribution of Key Variables")
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(figure_size=(14, 8))
)
```

```{python}
#| label: normality-tests
test_rows = []
for col, label in [
    ("total_usd", "CERF Allocation"),
    ("pop_exp_34kt", "Pop ≥ 34 kt"),
    ("pop_exp_50kt", "Pop ≥ 50 kt"),
    ("pop_exp_64kt", "Pop ≥ 64 kt"),
]:
    vals = merged_agg[col].dropna()
    vals = vals[vals > 0]
    if len(vals) < 8:
        continue

    sw_stat, sw_p = sp_stats.shapiro(vals)
    sw_stat_log, sw_p_log = sp_stats.shapiro(np.log10(vals))

    test_rows.append({
        "Variable": label,
        "n": len(vals),
        "Shapiro-Wilk (raw)": f"{sw_stat:.3f}",
        "p-value (raw)": f"{sw_p:.4f}",
        "Normal? (raw)": "Yes" if sw_p > 0.05 else "No",
        "Shapiro-Wilk (log)": f"{sw_stat_log:.3f}",
        "p-value (log)": f"{sw_p_log:.4f}",
        "Normal? (log)": "Yes" if sw_p_log > 0.05 else "No",
    })

normality_df = pd.DataFrame(test_rows)

(
    GT(normality_df)
    .tab_header(
        title="Shapiro-Wilk Normality Tests",
        subtitle="p > 0.05 indicates data is consistent with a normal distribution",
    )
    .tab_style(
        style=style.fill(color="#f4cccc"),
        locations=loc.body(
            columns="Normal? (raw)",
            rows=normality_df.index[normality_df["Normal? (raw)"] == "No"].tolist(),
        ),
    )
    .tab_style(
        style=style.fill(color="#d9ead3"),
        locations=loc.body(
            columns="Normal? (log)",
            rows=normality_df.index[normality_df["Normal? (log)"] == "Yes"].tolist(),
        ),
    )
    .tab_style(
        style=style.fill(color="#f4cccc"),
        locations=loc.body(
            columns="Normal? (log)",
            rows=normality_df.index[normality_df["Normal? (log)"] == "No"].tolist(),
        ),
    )
    .tab_source_note("Shapiro-Wilk test: H₀ = data is normally distributed")
)
```

```{python}
#| label: outlier-detection
print("Outliers per exposure column (IQR × 2 on log₁₀ scale):\n")
for col, label in [
    ("pop_exp_34kt", "≥ 34 kt"),
    ("pop_exp_50kt", "≥ 50 kt"),
    ("pop_exp_64kt", "≥ 64 kt"),
]:
    _, col_outliers = remove_col_outliers_iqr(merged_agg, col)
    print(f"  {label}: {len(col_outliers)} outlier(s)")
    for _, row in col_outliers.iterrows():
        storm = sid_name_map.get(row.get("sid", ""), "Unknown")
        print(
            f"    → {row['Country']} ({row['iso3']}, {storm}): "
            f"{col}={row[col]:,.0f}, CERF=${row['total_usd']:,.0f}"
        )
```

```{python}
#| label: normality-after-outliers
test_rows_clean = []
for col, label in [
    ("total_usd", "CERF Allocation"),
    ("pop_exp_34kt", "Pop ≥ 34 kt"),
    ("pop_exp_50kt", "Pop ≥ 50 kt"),
    ("pop_exp_64kt", "Pop ≥ 64 kt"),
]:
    if col == "total_usd":
        vals = merged_agg["total_usd"].dropna()
        vals = vals[vals > 0]
    else:
        clean, _ = remove_col_outliers_iqr(merged_agg, col)
        vals = clean[col]

    if len(vals) < 8:
        continue
    sw_stat_log, sw_p_log = sp_stats.shapiro(np.log10(vals))
    test_rows_clean.append({
        "Variable": label,
        "n": len(vals),
        "Shapiro-Wilk (log)": f"{sw_stat_log:.3f}",
        "p-value (log)": f"{sw_p_log:.4f}",
        "Normal? (log)": "Yes" if sw_p_log > 0.05 else "No",
    })

normality_clean_df = pd.DataFrame(test_rows_clean)

(
    GT(normality_clean_df)
    .tab_header(
        title="Shapiro-Wilk Tests After Per-Column Outlier Removal",
        subtitle="Each exposure column tested after removing its own outliers only",
    )
    .tab_style(
        style=style.fill(color="#d9ead3"),
        locations=loc.body(
            columns="Normal? (log)",
            rows=normality_clean_df.index[
                normality_clean_df["Normal? (log)"] == "Yes"
            ].tolist(),
        ),
    )
    .tab_style(
        style=style.fill(color="#f4cccc"),
        locations=loc.body(
            columns="Normal? (log)",
            rows=normality_clean_df.index[
                normality_clean_df["Normal? (log)"] == "No"
            ].tolist(),
        ),
    )
    .tab_source_note(
        "Each exposure variable tested after removing only that column's "
        "IQR outliers. CERF allocation is unchanged (no exposure outlier removal)."
    )
)
```

After per-column outlier removal, log-transformed distributions pass the
Shapiro-Wilk test, supporting **Pearson correlation on the log scale** in
addition to the distribution-free **Spearman rank correlation**.

## Objective 1: Predicting CERF Allocations from Exposure

Can cyclone population exposure alone predict how much CERF allocates to a
storm-affected country? We test this with bivariate correlations and OLS
regressions at three wind speed thresholds.

```{python}
#| label: prepare-subsets
#| output: false
merged_all = merged_agg.copy()
merged_2016 = merged_agg[merged_agg["allocation_year"] >= 2016].copy()

exp_meta = [
    ("pop_exp_34kt", "≥ 34 kt"),
    ("pop_exp_50kt", "≥ 50 kt"),
    ("pop_exp_64kt", "≥ 64 kt"),
]


def build_scatter_df(data):
    """Reshape to long format for plotnine, flagging per-column outliers."""
    frames = []
    for col, label in exp_meta:
        sub = data.dropna(subset=[col])
        sub = sub[(sub[col] > 0) & (sub["total_usd"] > 0)]
        _, outliers = remove_col_outliers_iqr(data, col)
        frame = sub[["Country", "total_usd"]].copy()
        frame["threshold"] = label
        frame["pop_exposed"] = sub[col]
        frame["is_outlier"] = frame.index.isin(outliers.index)
        frames.append(frame)
    df = pd.concat(frames, ignore_index=True)
    df["threshold"] = pd.Categorical(
        df["threshold"], categories=[l for _, l in exp_meta], ordered=True
    )
    return df


def build_annot_df(data):
    """Compute OLS r² and p-value annotations per facet."""
    rows = []
    for col, label in exp_meta:
        sub = data.dropna(subset=[col])
        sub = sub[(sub[col] > 0) & (sub["total_usd"] > 0)]
        log_x = np.log10(sub[col].values)
        log_y = np.log10(sub["total_usd"].values)
        if len(log_x) > 2:
            _, _, r, p, _ = sp_stats.linregress(log_x, log_y)
            rows.append({
                "threshold": label,
                "label": f"r²={r**2:.3f}, p={p:.4f}",
                "pop_exposed": 10 ** (log_x.min() + 0.05 * (log_x.max() - log_x.min())),
                "total_usd": 10 ** (log_y.max() - 0.05 * (log_y.max() - log_y.min())),
            })
    df = pd.DataFrame(rows)
    df["threshold"] = pd.Categorical(
        df["threshold"], categories=[l for _, l in exp_meta], ordered=True
    )
    return df
```

::: {.panel-tabset}

### All Data

```{python}
#| label: scatter-all
#| fig-cap: "All matched allocations: log-log scatter with OLS regression and 95% CI. Red circles mark outliers (per-column IQR on log scale)."
plot_df = build_scatter_df(merged_all)
annot_df = build_annot_df(merged_all)
outlier_df = plot_df[plot_df["is_outlier"]]

p = (
    ggplot(plot_df, aes("pop_exposed", "total_usd"))
    + geom_point(color="#4292c6", size=3, alpha=0.6)
    + (
        geom_point(
            data=outlier_df,
            color="red", size=5, stroke=1.5, shape="o", fill="none",
        )
        if len(outlier_df) > 0
        else geom_blank()
    )
    + geom_smooth(method="lm", se=True, color="red", fill="red", alpha=0.15)
    + geom_label(
        data=annot_df, mapping=aes(label="label"),
        ha="left", va="top", size=8, fill="white", alpha=0.8,
    )
    + scale_x_log10()
    + scale_y_log10()
    + facet_wrap("~threshold", scales="free")
    + labs(
        x="Population Exposed",
        y="CERF Allocation (USD)",
        title="All Data — Log Scale with OLS",
    )
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(figure_size=(16, 5))
)
p
```

```{python}
#| label: corr-all
corr_rows_all = []
for col, label in exp_meta:
    full = merged_all.dropna(subset=[col])
    full = full[(full[col] > 0) & (full["total_usd"] > 0)]

    clean, _ = remove_col_outliers_iqr(merged_all, col)

    for dset_label, subset in [("All data", full), ("Outliers removed", clean)]:
        if len(subset) < 3:
            continue
        r_log, p_log = sp_stats.pearsonr(
            np.log10(subset[col]), np.log10(subset["total_usd"])
        )
        rho, p_rho = sp_stats.spearmanr(subset[col], subset["total_usd"])
        corr_rows_all.append({
            "Threshold": label,
            "Dataset": dset_label,
            "n": len(subset),
            "r² (log)": f"{r_log**2:.3f}",
            "p (Pearson)": f"{p_log:.4f}",
            "Spearman rho": f"{rho:.3f}",
            "p (Spear.)": f"{p_rho:.4f}",
        })
corr_all_df = pd.DataFrame(corr_rows_all)

gt_all = (
    GT(corr_all_df)
    .tab_header(
        title="Correlation — All Data (CERF API)",
        subtitle="r² (log) matches scatter plot annotations; both full and cleaned data shown",
    )
    .tab_spanner(label="Pearson (log₁₀)", columns=["r² (log)", "p (Pearson)"])
    .tab_spanner(label="Spearman (rank)", columns=["Spearman rho", "p (Spear.)"])
)

clean_idx = corr_all_df.index[
    corr_all_df["Dataset"] == "Outliers removed"
].tolist()
if clean_idx:
    gt_all = gt_all.tab_style(
        style=style.fill(color="#f0f7f0"),
        locations=loc.body(rows=clean_idx),
    )

gt_all.tab_source_note(
    "r² = Pearson r squared on log₁₀ values (same statistic shown in scatter plots). "
    "Green rows = per-column IQR outliers removed."
)
```

### 2016+ (3RM Period)

Rost (2025) uses CERF allocations from 2016 onward for the 3RM model.
Filtering to this period substantially strengthens the exposure--allocation
relationship.

```{python}
#| label: scatter-2016
#| fig-cap: "2016+ allocations only: log-log scatter. Correlations are markedly stronger than the full time series."
plot_df = build_scatter_df(merged_2016)
annot_df = build_annot_df(merged_2016)
outlier_df = plot_df[plot_df["is_outlier"]]

p = (
    ggplot(plot_df, aes("pop_exposed", "total_usd"))
    + geom_point(color="#4292c6", size=3, alpha=0.6)
    + (
        geom_point(
            data=outlier_df,
            color="red", size=5, stroke=1.5, shape="o", fill="none",
        )
        if len(outlier_df) > 0
        else geom_blank()
    )
    + geom_smooth(method="lm", se=True, color="red", fill="red", alpha=0.15)
    + geom_label(
        data=annot_df, mapping=aes(label="label"),
        ha="left", va="top", size=8, fill="white", alpha=0.8,
    )
    + scale_x_log10()
    + scale_y_log10()
    + facet_wrap("~threshold", scales="free")
    + labs(
        x="Population Exposed",
        y="CERF Allocation (USD)",
        title="2016+ Allocations — Log Scale with OLS",
    )
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(figure_size=(16, 5))
)
p
```

```{python}
#| label: corr-2016
corr_rows_2016 = []
for col, label in exp_meta:
    full = merged_2016.dropna(subset=[col])
    full = full[(full[col] > 0) & (full["total_usd"] > 0)]

    clean, _ = remove_col_outliers_iqr(merged_2016, col)

    for dset_label, subset in [("All data", full), ("Outliers removed", clean)]:
        if len(subset) < 3:
            continue
        r_log, p_log = sp_stats.pearsonr(
            np.log10(subset[col]), np.log10(subset["total_usd"])
        )
        rho, p_rho = sp_stats.spearmanr(subset[col], subset["total_usd"])
        corr_rows_2016.append({
            "Threshold": label,
            "Dataset": dset_label,
            "n": len(subset),
            "r² (log)": f"{r_log**2:.3f}",
            "p (Pearson)": f"{p_log:.4f}",
            "Spearman rho": f"{rho:.3f}",
            "p (Spear.)": f"{p_rho:.4f}",
        })
corr_2016_df = pd.DataFrame(corr_rows_2016)

gt_2016 = (
    GT(corr_2016_df)
    .tab_header(
        title="Correlation — 2016+ (3RM Period)",
        subtitle="Restricted to allocations from 2016 onward, aligned with Rost (2025)",
    )
    .tab_spanner(label="Pearson (log₁₀)", columns=["r² (log)", "p (Pearson)"])
    .tab_spanner(label="Spearman (rank)", columns=["Spearman rho", "p (Spear.)"])
)

clean_idx = corr_2016_df.index[
    corr_2016_df["Dataset"] == "Outliers removed"
].tolist()
if clean_idx:
    gt_2016 = gt_2016.tab_style(
        style=style.fill(color="#f0f7f0"),
        locations=loc.body(rows=clean_idx),
    )

gt_2016.tab_source_note(
    "r² = Pearson r squared on log₁₀ values (same statistic shown in scatter plots). "
    "Green rows = per-column IQR outliers removed."
)
```

:::

### Exposure + CIRV

Does adding CIRV (the vulnerability index from Rost 2025) improve the
exposure-only model? This tests whether country-level vulnerability explains
allocation variance beyond what storm severity captures.

```{python}
#| label: cirv-setup
#| output: false
import statsmodels.api as sm
from src.datasets.cerf import load_3rm_cirv, load_cerf_api_data, apply_cerfcode_sids

# Load CIRV from blob (3RM dataset) and join via ApplicationCode
df_3rm = load_3rm_cirv()
cirv_map = dict(zip(df_3rm["ApplicationCode"], df_3rm["CIRV"]))

# Get ApplicationCode -> (sid, iso3) from our pipeline to bridge the join
df_api_raw = load_cerf_api_data()
df_api_matched = apply_cerfcode_sids(df_api_raw)
df_api_matched["CIRV"] = df_api_matched["ApplicationCode"].map(cirv_map)

# Build sid+iso3 -> CIRV lookup (CIRV is constant per country-year,
# so taking first value per group is safe)
cirv_by_pair = (
    df_api_matched.dropna(subset=["sid", "CIRV"])
    .groupby(["sid", "iso3"])["CIRV"]
    .first()
    .reset_index()
)

merged_cirv = merged_agg.merge(cirv_by_pair, on=["sid", "iso3"], how="left")
print(f"CIRV coverage: {merged_cirv['CIRV'].notna().sum()} of {len(merged_cirv)} rows")

# Filter to rows with CIRV available and positive allocation
merged_cirv_pos = merged_cirv[merged_cirv["total_usd"] > 0].copy()

# Build per-threshold regression datasets (all data and outlier-removed)
reg_data = {}
for col, label in exp_meta:
    sub = merged_cirv_pos.dropna(subset=[col, "CIRV"])
    sub = sub[sub[col] > 0].copy()
    sub["log_exp"] = np.log10(sub[col])
    sub["log_usd"] = np.log10(sub["total_usd"])
    sub["log_cirv"] = np.log10(sub["CIRV"])
    clean, outliers = remove_col_outliers_iqr(sub, col)
    clean["log_exp"] = np.log10(clean[col])
    clean["log_usd"] = np.log10(clean["total_usd"])
    clean["log_cirv"] = np.log10(clean["CIRV"])
    reg_data[label] = {"all": sub, "clean": clean, "outliers": outliers, "col": col}
```

```{python}
#| label: cirv-scatter
#| fig-cap: "CERF allocation vs population exposure at each wind speed threshold, coloured by CIRV score. Red circles mark IQR outliers on log exposure."
scatter_frames = []
outlier_frames = []
for label, rd in reg_data.items():
    frame = rd["all"][["Country", "total_usd", "CIRV"]].copy()
    frame["threshold"] = label
    frame["pop_exposed"] = rd["all"][rd["col"]]
    scatter_frames.append(frame)
    if len(rd["outliers"]) > 0:
        oframe = rd["outliers"][["Country", "total_usd", "CIRV"]].copy()
        oframe["threshold"] = label
        oframe["pop_exposed"] = rd["outliers"][rd["col"]]
        outlier_frames.append(oframe)

scatter_df = pd.concat(scatter_frames, ignore_index=True)
scatter_df["threshold"] = pd.Categorical(
    scatter_df["threshold"], categories=[l for _, l in exp_meta], ordered=True
)
outlier_df_cirv = pd.concat(outlier_frames, ignore_index=True) if outlier_frames else pd.DataFrame()
if len(outlier_df_cirv) > 0:
    outlier_df_cirv["threshold"] = pd.Categorical(
        outlier_df_cirv["threshold"], categories=[l for _, l in exp_meta], ordered=True
    )

(
    ggplot(scatter_df, aes("pop_exposed", "total_usd", color="CIRV"))
    + geom_point(size=3, alpha=0.8)
    + (
        geom_point(
            data=outlier_df_cirv,
            color="red", size=5, stroke=1.5, shape="o", fill="none",
        )
        if len(outlier_df_cirv) > 0
        else geom_blank()
    )
    + geom_smooth(method="lm", se=True, color="red", fill="red", alpha=0.15)
    + scale_x_log10()
    + scale_y_log10()
    + scale_color_gradient(low="#fee8c8", high="#b30000", name="CIRV")
    + facet_wrap("~threshold", scales="free")
    + labs(
        x="Population Exposed",
        y="CERF Allocation (USD)",
        title="Exposure vs Allocation — Coloured by CIRV (2016+)",
    )
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(figure_size=(16, 5))
)
```

```{python}
#| label: cirv-regression-table
model_rows = []

for label, rd in reg_data.items():
    df_all = rd["all"]
    df_clean = rd["clean"]

    # Exposure only
    X1 = sm.add_constant(df_all[["log_exp"]])
    m1 = sm.OLS(df_all["log_usd"], X1).fit()
    model_rows.append({
        "Threshold": label,
        "Model": "Exposure only",
        "n": int(m1.nobs),
        "R²": f"{m1.rsquared:.3f}",
        "Adj R²": f"{m1.rsquared_adj:.3f}",
        "p (exposure)": f"{m1.pvalues['log_exp']:.4f}",
        "p (CIRV)": "—",
    })

    # CIRV only
    X2 = sm.add_constant(df_all[["log_cirv"]])
    m2 = sm.OLS(df_all["log_usd"], X2).fit()
    model_rows.append({
        "Threshold": label,
        "Model": "CIRV only",
        "n": int(m2.nobs),
        "R²": f"{m2.rsquared:.3f}",
        "Adj R²": f"{m2.rsquared_adj:.3f}",
        "p (exposure)": "—",
        "p (CIRV)": f"{m2.pvalues['log_cirv']:.4f}",
    })

    # Exposure + CIRV
    X3 = sm.add_constant(df_all[["log_exp", "log_cirv"]])
    m3 = sm.OLS(df_all["log_usd"], X3).fit()
    model_rows.append({
        "Threshold": label,
        "Model": "Exposure + CIRV",
        "n": int(m3.nobs),
        "R²": f"{m3.rsquared:.3f}",
        "Adj R²": f"{m3.rsquared_adj:.3f}",
        "p (exposure)": f"{m3.pvalues['log_exp']:.4f}",
        "p (CIRV)": f"{m3.pvalues['log_cirv']:.4f}",
    })

    # Exposure only (outliers removed)
    if len(df_clean) >= 5:
        X4a = sm.add_constant(df_clean[["log_exp"]])
        m4a = sm.OLS(df_clean["log_usd"], X4a).fit()
        model_rows.append({
            "Threshold": label,
            "Model": "Exposure only (outliers removed)",
            "n": int(m4a.nobs),
            "R²": f"{m4a.rsquared:.3f}",
            "Adj R²": f"{m4a.rsquared_adj:.3f}",
            "p (exposure)": f"{m4a.pvalues['log_exp']:.4f}",
            "p (CIRV)": "—",
        })

    # Exposure + CIRV (outliers removed)
    if len(df_clean) >= 5:
        X4 = sm.add_constant(df_clean[["log_exp", "log_cirv"]])
        m4 = sm.OLS(df_clean["log_usd"], X4).fit()
        model_rows.append({
            "Threshold": label,
            "Model": "Exposure + CIRV (outliers removed)",
            "n": int(m4.nobs),
            "R²": f"{m4.rsquared:.3f}",
            "Adj R²": f"{m4.rsquared_adj:.3f}",
            "p (exposure)": f"{m4.pvalues['log_exp']:.4f}",
            "p (CIRV)": f"{m4.pvalues['log_cirv']:.4f}",
        })

model_df = pd.DataFrame(model_rows)

combined_idx = model_df.index[model_df["Model"] == "Exposure + CIRV"].tolist()
clean_idx = model_df.index[model_df["Model"].str.contains("outliers removed")].tolist()

(
    GT(model_df)
    .tab_header(
        title="OLS Regression Comparison (2016+)",
        subtitle="Dependent variable: log₁₀(CERF allocation USD)",
    )
    .tab_spanner(label="Fit", columns=["R²", "Adj R²"])
    .tab_spanner(label="Significance", columns=["p (exposure)", "p (CIRV)"])
    .tab_style(
        style=style.fill(color="#e6eeff"),
        locations=loc.body(rows=combined_idx),
    )
    .tab_style(
        style=style.fill(color="#f0f7f0"),
        locations=loc.body(rows=clean_idx),
    )
    .tab_source_note(
        "All variables log₁₀-transformed. CIRV from Rost (2025) 3RM dataset. "
        "Blue rows = combined model. Green rows = combined with IQR outliers removed."
    )
)
```

**CIRV adds little on top of exposure** for storm allocations: at 64 kt with
outliers removed, adding CIRV nudges R² up slightly while Adj R² decreases,
and CIRV itself is not significant. Hazard-specific exposure captures more
allocation-relevant information than the generic vulnerability index.

## Objective 2: Predicting 3RM Outcome Variables from Exposure

Beyond allocation amounts, the 3RM dataset contains other outcome variables
that characterise crisis response. Can cyclone exposure predict these? We
test bivariate regressions (exposure only) for three outcomes:

- **Funding required** (`LogRequired`, ln total amount required)
- **People targeted** (`LogTargeted`, ln total individuals targeted)
- **People affected** (`Total Individuals Affected`, we apply ln-transform)

Only TC allocations with matched exposure data are included (n = 40).

```{python}
#| label: 3rm-outcome-setup
#| output: false
from src.datasets.cerf import CERFCODE_TO_SID

# Load full 3RM MergedData (not just CIRV)
raw_3rm = stratus.load_blob_data(
    "ds-storm-impact-harmonisation/raw/"
    "CERF 3RM - RR Regression Model - version 1.8.xlsx"
)
df_3rm_full = pd.read_excel(BytesIO(raw_3rm), sheet_name="MergedData")
df_3rm_full = df_3rm_full.rename(columns={"Application Code": "ApplicationCode"})

# Keep only TC allocations
tc_codes = {k for k, v in CERFCODE_TO_SID.items() if v is not None}
df_3rm_tc = df_3rm_full[df_3rm_full["ApplicationCode"].isin(tc_codes)].copy()
df_3rm_tc["sid"] = df_3rm_tc["ApplicationCode"].map(CERFCODE_TO_SID)

# Get iso3 from CERF API data
code_to_iso3 = dict(zip(df_api_matched["ApplicationCode"], df_api_matched["iso3"]))
df_3rm_tc["iso3"] = df_3rm_tc["ApplicationCode"].map(code_to_iso3)

# Merge with OCHA exposure
from src.datasets.cerf import pivot_ocha_wide

ocha_wide = pivot_ocha_wide(df_ocha)
df_3rm_exp = df_3rm_tc.merge(ocha_wide, on=["sid", "iso3"], how="inner")

# Add ln-transformed affected
df_3rm_exp["LogAffected"] = np.log(
    df_3rm_exp["Total Individuals Affected"].replace(0, np.nan)
)

print(f"TC allocations in 3RM with exposure data: {len(df_3rm_exp)}")
for col in ["LogRequired", "LogTargeted", "LogAffected"]:
    print(f"  {col}: {df_3rm_exp[col].notna().sum()} non-null")
```

```{python}
#| label: 3rm-outcome-models
outcome_meta = [
    ("LogRequired", "ln(Funding Required)"),
    ("LogTargeted", "ln(People Targeted)"),
    ("LogAffected", "ln(People Affected)"),
]

model_rows_3rm = []
for outcome_col, outcome_label in outcome_meta:
    for exp_col, exp_label in exp_meta:
        sub = df_3rm_exp.dropna(subset=[exp_col, outcome_col])
        sub = sub[sub[exp_col] > 0].copy()
        if len(sub) < 5:
            continue
        sub["log_exp"] = np.log(sub[exp_col])
        X = sm.add_constant(sub[["log_exp"]])
        m = sm.OLS(sub[outcome_col], X).fit()
        model_rows_3rm.append({
            "Outcome": outcome_label,
            "Predictor": f"ln(Pop {exp_label})",
            "n": int(m.nobs),
            "R²": round(m.rsquared, 3),
            "Adj R²": round(m.rsquared_adj, 3),
            "β (exposure)": round(m.params["log_exp"], 3),
            "p (exposure)": round(m.pvalues["log_exp"], 4),
        })

model_3rm_df = pd.DataFrame(model_rows_3rm)

sig_idx = model_3rm_df.index[model_3rm_df["p (exposure)"] < 0.05].tolist()

(
    GT(model_3rm_df)
    .tab_header(
        title="OLS: Cyclone Exposure → 3RM Outcomes (TC Allocations Only)",
        subtitle="Each row is a separate bivariate regression. All variables ln-transformed.",
    )
    .tab_spanner(label="Fit", columns=["R²", "Adj R²"])
    .tab_spanner(label="Exposure coefficient", columns=["β (exposure)", "p (exposure)"])
    .tab_style(
        style=style.fill(color="#d9ead3"),
        locations=loc.body(rows=sig_idx),
    )
    .tab_source_note(
        "Green rows: p < 0.05. β = elasticity: a 1% increase in exposed "
        "population is associated with a β% change in the outcome."
    )
)
```

```{python}
#| label: 3rm-outcome-scatter
#| fig-cap: "Cyclone population exposure vs 3RM outcomes (TC allocations only). Each column is a wind speed threshold; each row is an outcome variable."
scatter_rows = []
for outcome_col, outcome_label in outcome_meta:
    for exp_col, exp_label in exp_meta:
        sub = df_3rm_exp.dropna(subset=[exp_col, outcome_col])
        sub = sub[sub[exp_col] > 0].copy()
        for _, row in sub.iterrows():
            scatter_rows.append({
                "outcome": outcome_label,
                "threshold": exp_label,
                "pop_exposed": row[exp_col],
                "outcome_value": row[outcome_col],
            })

scatter_3rm = pd.DataFrame(scatter_rows)
scatter_3rm["threshold"] = pd.Categorical(
    scatter_3rm["threshold"],
    categories=[l for _, l in exp_meta],
    ordered=True,
)
scatter_3rm["outcome"] = pd.Categorical(
    scatter_3rm["outcome"],
    categories=[l for _, l in outcome_meta],
    ordered=True,
)

(
    ggplot(scatter_3rm, aes("pop_exposed", "outcome_value"))
    + geom_point(color="#4292c6", size=2.5, alpha=0.7)
    + geom_smooth(method="lm", se=True, color="red", fill="red", alpha=0.15)
    + scale_x_log10()
    + facet_grid("outcome ~ threshold", scales="free")
    + labs(
        x="Population Exposed (log scale)",
        y="Outcome (ln-transformed)",
        title="Exposure vs 3RM Outcomes — TC Allocations",
    )
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(figure_size=(14, 10))
)
```

## Objective 3: Extending the 3RM Model with Exposure {#sec-3rm-extension}

Rost (2025) presents a regression model (3RM) that predicts CERF allocation
amounts using four factors: emergency type, CIRV, total funding required, and
people targeted. The paper notes that hazard-specific severity data (such as
cyclone wind speed) is not yet included. Here we test whether adding cyclone
population exposure improves the model for storm allocations.

### Replicating the Published Model

We first verify that we can reproduce the published Model 1 coefficients using
the 3RM dataset (v1.8). The paper used allocations from January 2016 -- July
2024 (n = 378); our dataset has been updated since publication, yielding
slightly more rows for the same period.

```{python}
#| label: 3rm-replicate
#| output: false
def _stars(p):
    if p < 0.01:
        return "***"
    if p < 0.05:
        return "**"
    if p < 0.1:
        return "*"
    return ""

def _fmt_coef(model, var):
    """Format as coefficient with stars + p-value in parentheses."""
    b = model.params[var]
    p = model.pvalues[var]
    return f"{b:.3f}{_stars(p)} ({p:.2f})"

# Load full 3RM dataset (already loaded as df_3rm_full above)
df_3rm_full["date"] = pd.to_datetime(df_3rm_full["Date of Most Recent Submission"])

# Paper period: Jan 2016 – Jul 2024
df_paper = df_3rm_full[df_3rm_full["date"] <= "2024-07-31"].copy()

etype_cols = [
    "Storm", "Flood", "Drought", "OtherNatural",
    "Cholera", "Ebola", "OtherHealth", "DisplConfl",
]
base_3rm_vars = etype_cols + ["CIRV", "LogRequired", "LogTargeted"]
```

```{python}
#| label: 3rm-replicate-table
X_paper = sm.add_constant(df_paper[base_3rm_vars].astype(float))
m_paper = sm.OLS(df_paper["LogApproved"], X_paper).fit()

paper_coefs = {
    "const": 8.43, "Storm": -0.28, "Flood": -0.30, "Drought": -0.24,
    "OtherNatural": -0.32, "Cholera": -0.99, "Ebola": -0.71,
    "OtherHealth": -1.39, "DisplConfl": -0.01, "CIRV": 0.01,
    "LogRequired": 0.20, "LogTargeted": 0.25,
}

paper_pvals = {
    "const": 0.00, "Storm": 0.12, "Flood": 0.09, "Drought": 0.18,
    "OtherNatural": 0.11, "Cholera": 0.00, "Ebola": 0.00,
    "OtherHealth": 0.00, "DisplConfl": 0.97, "CIRV": 0.00,
    "LogRequired": 0.00, "LogTargeted": 0.00,
}

rep_rows = []
for var in m_paper.params.index:
    pc = paper_coefs.get(var, "—")
    pp = paper_pvals.get(var, 1.0)
    rep_rows.append({
        "Variable": var.replace("const", "Intercept"),
        "Rost (2025)": f"{pc}{_stars(pp)} ({pp:.2f})",
        "Our replication": _fmt_coef(m_paper, var),
    })
rep_rows.append({"Variable": "n", "Rost (2025)": "378", "Our replication": str(int(m_paper.nobs))})
rep_rows.append({"Variable": "Adj R²", "Rost (2025)": "0.71", "Our replication": f"{m_paper.rsquared_adj:.3f}"})

(
    GT(pd.DataFrame(rep_rows))
    .tab_header(
        title="3RM Model 1 Replication",
        subtitle="Rost (2025) vs our reproduction using 3RM v1.8 (≤ Jul 2024)",
    )
    .tab_style(style=style.css("font-size: 0.85em;"), locations=loc.body())
    .tab_style(style=style.css("font-size: 0.85em;"), locations=loc.column_labels())
    .tab_source_note(
        "Outcome: ln(CERF allocation). Base category: 'Other' emergency type. "
        "*** p < 0.01, ** p < 0.05, * p < 0.1. "
        "Small differences due to dataset updates since publication."
    )
)
```

Coefficients match closely. The 10-row difference reflects dataset updates
(new allocations or corrections added to v1.8 after the paper was finalised).

### Full Updated Dataset

Running the same model specification on the complete v1.8 dataset
(2016--2025):

```{python}
#| label: 3rm-full-update
X_full = sm.add_constant(df_3rm_full[base_3rm_vars].astype(float))
m_full = sm.OLS(df_3rm_full["LogApproved"], X_full).fit()

update_rows = []
for var in m_full.params.index:
    update_rows.append({
        "Variable": var.replace("const", "Intercept"),
        "Paper period": _fmt_coef(m_paper, var),
        "Full v1.8": _fmt_coef(m_full, var),
    })
update_rows.append({"Variable": "n", "Paper period": str(int(m_paper.nobs)), "Full v1.8": str(int(m_full.nobs))})
update_rows.append({"Variable": "Adj R²", "Paper period": f"{m_paper.rsquared_adj:.3f}", "Full v1.8": f"{m_full.rsquared_adj:.3f}"})

(
    GT(pd.DataFrame(update_rows))
    .tab_header(
        title="3RM Model 1 — Paper Period vs Full Updated Dataset",
        subtitle="Coefficient (p-value) for each specification",
    )
    .tab_style(style=style.css("font-size: 0.85em;"), locations=loc.body())
    .tab_style(style=style.css("font-size: 0.85em;"), locations=loc.column_labels())
    .tab_source_note(
        "*** p < 0.01, ** p < 0.05, * p < 0.1. "
        "Full v1.8 includes 2025 allocations added since publication."
    )
)
```

### Adding Cyclone Exposure to 3RM

For the TC-only subset (Storm = 1) with matched exposure data, emergency
type dummies are constant and drop out. We test two model families, each
with and without CIRV (which is not significant for TC allocations):

- **Model A**: People targeted included (full 3RM specification for storms)
- **Model B**: People targeted excluded (mirroring Rost's Model 2 for
  scenarios where targeting data is unavailable)

```{python}
#| label: 3rm-tc-exposure

def build_model_table(base_vars, title, data):
    """Build a variables-as-rows, models-as-columns comparison table."""
    all_var_labels = [
        ("const", "Intercept"),
        ("CIRV", "CIRV"),
        ("LogRequired", "Funding req. (ln)"),
        ("LogTargeted", "People targeted (ln)"),
        ("ln_exp", "Pop. exposed (ln)"),
    ]

    X_b = sm.add_constant(data[base_vars].astype(float))
    m_base = sm.OLS(data["LogApproved"], X_b).fit()
    models = {"Base": m_base}

    for exp_col, exp_label in exp_meta:
        sub = data.dropna(subset=[exp_col])
        sub = sub[sub[exp_col] > 0].copy()
        sub["ln_exp"] = np.log(sub[exp_col])
        X_a = sm.add_constant(sub[base_vars + ["ln_exp"]].astype(float))
        models[f"+ {exp_label}"] = sm.OLS(sub["LogApproved"], X_a).fit()

    tbl_rows = []
    for var_key, var_label in all_var_labels:
        row = {"Variable": var_label}
        for mname, m in models.items():
            if var_key in m.params.index:
                row[mname] = _fmt_coef(m, var_key)
            elif var_key in base_vars or var_key == "ln_exp":
                row[mname] = "—"
            else:
                row[mname] = ""
        vals = [row[k] for k in models]
        if all(v == "" for v in vals):
            continue
        tbl_rows.append(row)

    n_row = {"Variable": "n"}
    r2_row = {"Variable": "Adj R²"}
    for mname, m in models.items():
        n_row[mname] = str(int(m.nobs))
        r2_row[mname] = f"{m.rsquared_adj:.3f}"
    tbl_rows.extend([n_row, r2_row])

    tbl_df = pd.DataFrame(tbl_rows)
    exp_idx = tbl_df.index[tbl_df["Variable"] == "Pop. exposed (ln)"].tolist()

    return (
        GT(tbl_df)
        .tab_header(title=title, subtitle="Coefficient (p-value)")
        .tab_style(style=style.fill(color="#e6eeff"), locations=loc.body(rows=exp_idx))
        .tab_style(style=style.css("font-size: 0.85em;"), locations=loc.body())
        .tab_style(style=style.css("font-size: 0.85em;"), locations=loc.column_labels())
        .tab_source_note(
            "Outcome: ln(CERF allocation). Blue row = exposure variable. "
            "*** p < 0.01, ** p < 0.05, * p < 0.1."
        )
    )
```

#### Model A: People targeted included

```{python}
#| label: 3rm-model-a1
build_model_table(
    ["CIRV", "LogRequired", "LogTargeted"],
    "Model A1: With CIRV",
    df_3rm_exp,
)
```

```{python}
#| label: 3rm-model-a2
build_model_table(
    ["LogRequired", "LogTargeted"],
    "Model A2: Without CIRV",
    df_3rm_exp,
)
```

#### Model B: People targeted excluded

Rost (2025) provides an alternative model (Model 2) for cases where the
number of people targeted is not yet available. Removing LogTargeted weakens the base model, giving
exposure more room to contribute.

```{python}
#| label: 3rm-model-b1
build_model_table(
    ["CIRV", "LogRequired"],
    "Model B1: With CIRV",
    df_3rm_exp,
)
```

```{python}
#| label: 3rm-model-b2
build_model_table(
    ["LogRequired"],
    "Model B2: Without CIRV",
    df_3rm_exp,
)
```

#### Interpretation

Several patterns emerge across the four specifications:

- **CIRV is not significant for storms** in any specification. In the full
  3RM (all emergency types, n ≈ 400), CIRV is highly significant because it
  captures background vulnerability that varies greatly across emergency
  types. Within TC-only allocations, CIRV adds nothing; dropping it
  (A2 vs A1, B2 vs B1) leaves Adj R² essentially unchanged.
- **Exposure shows the largest Adj R² gains when LogTargeted is absent**
  (Model B). Without the people-targeted variable, the base model is weaker
  and exposure has more room to explain variance. However, exposure
  coefficients remain non-significant even in these simplified models.
- **Adj R² can increase while the exposure p-value remains non-significant.**
  R² mechanically increases whenever a predictor is added; Adj R² penalises
  for the extra parameter but weakly at small n. The p-value tests whether
  the coefficient is distinguishable from zero, and with only 29--40
  observations, statistical power is low. A genuine but modest effect can
  improve fit without reaching conventional significance. This is a sample
  size limitation, not evidence of absence.
- **LogRequired and LogTargeted dominate** when both are present. Countries
  with more people in the path of a cyclone tend to submit larger funding
  requests and target more beneficiaries, so these variables already absorb
  much of the severity signal that exposure provides.

In summary, while exposure captures meaningful variation in storm severity
(as shown by its ability to predict LogRequired and Total Individuals
Affected in Objective 2), it does not translate into significant predictive
power for CERF allocation amounts, whether used alone or as an addition
to the 3RM model.

## Key Findings

### Objective 1: Exposure → CERF allocation amounts

- **Strongest signal at 64 kt**: Population exposed to >= 64 kt winds shows
  the strongest correlation with CERF allocation amounts.
- **Stronger signal from 2016 onward**: Restricting to the 3RM period
  (2016+) strengthens correlations across all thresholds.
- **Outlier removal matters**: Removing per-column IQR outliers substantially
  improves exposure's predictive power and restores log-normality.
- **CIRV adds little for storms**: Hazard-specific exposure captures more
  allocation-relevant information than the generic vulnerability index.

### Objective 2: Exposure → 3RM outcome variables

- Wind-speed exposure significantly predicts **funding required** and
  **people affected** for TC allocations, with the 64 kt threshold again
  showing the strongest relationship.
- **People targeted** shows a weaker relationship with exposure, possibly
  because targeting reflects operational capacity constraints rather than
  storm severity alone.

### Objective 3: Extending the 3RM with exposure

- **3RM replication**: The published 3RM (Rost 2025, Adj R² = 0.71)
  reproduces closely with the updated v1.8 dataset.
- **CIRV is not significant** for storm-only allocations in any specification
  and can be dropped without loss of fit.
- **Exposure contributes most when LogTargeted is absent** (Model B), showing
  the largest Adj R² gains, though coefficients remain non-significant.
- **Small sample size limits significance**: With only 29--40 TC allocations,
  exposure can improve Adj R² while remaining non-significant. This is a power
  limitation, not evidence of absence.
- **Bottom line**: Exposure captures storm severity but does not significantly
  predict CERF allocation amounts in any model specification tested.

## Appendix: Objective 1 Expansion -- Feature Engineering

CIRV performed poorly as a vulnerability proxy for TC allocations. Can we
construct a better country-level feature from the CERF data itself? We test
two variants of historical average Rapid Response spending per country as
a predictor of TC allocation amounts.

Note: both features involve data leakage since the outcome allocation
contributes (directly or indirectly) to the country average. We use a
leave-one-out (LOO) mean to partially mitigate this, but the results
should be interpreted as exploratory upper bounds rather than validated
predictions.

```{python}
#| label: feat-eng-setup
#| output: false
from src.datasets.cerf import load_cerf_api_data
import statsmodels.api as sm

# TC-only LOO mean: for each row, avg of all OTHER TC allocations for that country
def loo_mean(group):
    total = group["total_usd"].sum()
    n = len(group)
    if n <= 1:
        return pd.Series([np.nan] * n, index=group.index)
    return (total - group["total_usd"]) / (n - 1)

df_fe = merged_2016.copy()
df_fe["tc_avg_rr_loo"] = df_fe.groupby("iso3", group_keys=False).apply(loo_mean)

# All-type avg annual RR: country-level mean annual RR spending across all emergency types
df_cerf_all_rr = load_cerf_api_data(emergency_type=None)
yearly_rr = (
    df_cerf_all_rr
    .groupby(["CountryCode", "Year"])["TotalAmountApproved"]
    .sum()
    .reset_index()
)
country_avg_annual_rr = (
    yearly_rr
    .groupby("CountryCode")["TotalAmountApproved"]
    .mean()
    .reset_index()
    .rename(columns={"TotalAmountApproved": "all_avg_annual_rr", "CountryCode": "iso3"})
)
df_fe = df_fe.merge(country_avg_annual_rr, on="iso3", how="left")
```

### Feature 1: TC-Only Average RR (Leave-One-Out)

For each storm-country allocation, we compute the average of all *other*
TC allocations to the same country (excluding the current one). Countries
with only a single TC allocation are dropped.

```{python}
#| label: feat-tc-loo
df_tc_loo = df_fe.dropna(subset=["tc_avg_rr_loo"]).copy()
df_tc_loo = df_tc_loo[df_tc_loo["total_usd"] > 0].copy()
df_tc_loo["ln_alloc"] = np.log(df_tc_loo["total_usd"])
df_tc_loo["ln_tc_avg"] = np.log(df_tc_loo["tc_avg_rr_loo"])

print(f"n = {len(df_tc_loo)} (countries with >1 TC allocation: {df_tc_loo['iso3'].nunique()})")

X = sm.add_constant(df_tc_loo[["ln_tc_avg"]])
m = sm.OLS(df_tc_loo["ln_alloc"], X).fit()

rows = []
for col, label in exp_meta:
    sub = df_tc_loo.dropna(subset=[col])
    sub = sub[sub[col] > 0].copy()
    sub["ln_exp"] = np.log(sub[col])

    me = sm.OLS(sub["ln_alloc"], sm.add_constant(sub[["ln_exp"]])).fit()
    ma = sm.OLS(sub["ln_alloc"], sm.add_constant(sub[["ln_tc_avg"]])).fit()
    mb = sm.OLS(sub["ln_alloc"], sm.add_constant(sub[["ln_tc_avg", "ln_exp"]])).fit()

    rows.append({
        "Threshold": label, "n": len(sub),
        "Exposure only": f"{me.rsquared_adj:.3f}",
        "TC avg RR only": f"{ma.rsquared_adj:.3f}",
        "Both": f"{mb.rsquared_adj:.3f}",
        "p (TC avg)": f"{mb.pvalues['ln_tc_avg']:.3f}{_stars(mb.pvalues['ln_tc_avg'])}",
        "p (exposure)": f"{mb.pvalues['ln_exp']:.3f}{_stars(mb.pvalues['ln_exp'])}",
    })

(
    GT(pd.DataFrame(rows))
    .tab_header(
        title="TC-Only Average RR (LOO) as Predictor",
        subtitle="Adj R² for ln(allocation) ~ predictors, 2016+ TC allocations",
    )
)
```

The TC-only LOO mean has no predictive power (Adj R² near zero). A
country's past TC allocation levels do not predict the next one, likely
because TC impacts vary too much from storm to storm.

### Feature 2: All-Type Average Annual RR

Instead of TC-only history, we use each country's average *annual* Rapid
Response spending across all emergency types as a proxy for overall crisis
exposure and CERF engagement. This captures country-level factors like
economy size, disaster frequency, and institutional relationship with CERF.

```{python}
#| label: feat-all-rr
df_all_rr = df_fe.dropna(subset=["all_avg_annual_rr"]).copy()
df_all_rr = df_all_rr[df_all_rr["total_usd"] > 0].copy()
df_all_rr["ln_alloc"] = np.log(df_all_rr["total_usd"])
df_all_rr["ln_all_avg"] = np.log(df_all_rr["all_avg_annual_rr"])

print(f"n = {len(df_all_rr)}, countries = {df_all_rr['iso3'].nunique()}")

rows = []
for col, label in exp_meta:
    sub = df_all_rr.dropna(subset=[col])
    sub = sub[sub[col] > 0].copy()
    sub["ln_exp"] = np.log(sub[col])

    me = sm.OLS(sub["ln_alloc"], sm.add_constant(sub[["ln_exp"]])).fit()
    ma = sm.OLS(sub["ln_alloc"], sm.add_constant(sub[["ln_all_avg"]])).fit()
    mb = sm.OLS(sub["ln_alloc"], sm.add_constant(sub[["ln_all_avg", "ln_exp"]])).fit()

    rows.append({
        "Threshold": label, "n": len(sub),
        "Exposure only": f"{me.rsquared_adj:.3f}",
        "All-type avg RR only": f"{ma.rsquared_adj:.3f}",
        "Both": f"{mb.rsquared_adj:.3f}",
        "p (avg RR)": f"{mb.pvalues['ln_all_avg']:.3f}{_stars(mb.pvalues['ln_all_avg'])}",
        "p (exposure)": f"{mb.pvalues['ln_exp']:.3f}{_stars(mb.pvalues['ln_exp'])}",
    })

(
    GT(pd.DataFrame(rows))
    .tab_header(
        title="All-Type Average Annual RR as Predictor",
        subtitle="Adj R² for ln(allocation) ~ predictors, 2016+ TC allocations",
    )
)
```

Average annual RR across all emergency types is a strong, significant
predictor on its own, outperforming both CIRV and wind-speed exposure as
a standalone predictor of TC allocation amounts across all thresholds
(see table above). However, the data
leakage caveat applies: each country's TC allocations contribute to its
own average, inflating the apparent predictive power. Note that the average
is computed as a single country-level value (mean annual RR across all
years and emergency types), not joined per-year, so every TC allocation
for a given country receives the same predictor value.

### Note: Cyclone-Specific Vulnerability Indices

We explored whether an external cyclone-specific vulnerability index could
serve as a better country-level predictor than CIRV. No ready-to-use,
cyclone-specific socioeconomic vulnerability index exists at the country
level. The closest options are:

- **INFORM Risk Index**: What CIRV is derived from. Already tested in this
  analysis; not significant for TC allocations.
- **Basnayake et al. (2026) Coastal Vulnerability Index**: A global
  assessment of coastal vulnerability to erosion and flooding
  (doi:10.1038/s41467-025-67275-6). Purely geophysical (coastal slope,
  geomorphology, tidal range, wave height, sea level change, shoreline
  change). No socioeconomic component, so unlikely to predict allocation
  decisions.
- **TCE-DAT**: Global tropical cyclone exposure dataset (1950--2015) with
  population and GDP exposed to wind thresholds. Similar to the OCHA
  exposure data we already use, but adds an asset/GDP dimension.
- **CLIMADA**: Open-source risk modeling platform that could produce
  country-level TC risk estimates, but requires running the model rather
  than providing a precomputed index.
- **ND-GAIN**: Climate adaptation vulnerability index. Country level, 192
  countries. Not cyclone-specific but more climate-focused than INFORM.

The finding that CERF's own historical RR spending outperforms all
external indices as a vulnerability proxy suggests that the factors driving
allocation decisions (crisis frequency, government capacity, CERF
relationship) are better captured by revealed spending patterns than by
any single external index.
